content.text: Four libraries dominate modern frontend development: React, Angular, Vue, and Svelte. They all solve the same core problem — building interactive UIs — but with different philosophies about how much to prescribe, how reactivity works, and what the developer experience should feel like. Understanding these differences helps you choose the right tool and explains why React's particular approach is worth learning first.

attrs.alt: React vs Angular vs Vue vs Svelte comparison
attrs.title: Four frameworks compared

content.text: . React is deliberately just a UI library — it renders components and manages state, but routing, form handling, data fetching, and HTTP are all left to you. Angular sits at the opposite end: it is a full framework with official solutions for every concern, enforced through a module system, dependency injection, and decorators. Vue and Svelte sit in between, offering optional officiality — you can use their blessed add-ons or swap them out.
React's minimal core is a feature, not a gap. It means Facebook, Netflix, and a solo developer's side project can all use React while making completely different infrastructure choices.

React was created by Meta (then Facebook) in 2013 and open-sourced later that year. Its central insight was that UI should be a pure function of state: given the same props and state, a component always produces the same output. This makes UIs predictable and easier to test.
— JavaScript extended with XML-like syntax that compiles to plain
calls. Because it is just JavaScript, the full language is available inside templates with no special directives required.
Virtually every UI pattern has a battle-tested library: React Query, React Hook Form, Zustand, Jotai, React Router, and thousands more.
React is the most-requested skill in frontend job postings by a significant margin.
The same component model targets iOS and Android, meaning React knowledge transfers to mobile development.
React 18–19 introduced React Server Components (RSC), allowing components to run on the server and stream HTML without shipping their dependencies to the client.
Next.js and Remix sit on top of React and add file-based routing, SSR, SSG, and data-loading conventions when you need them.

React's main friction points: the number of choices can overwhelm beginners (which state library? which router? which data layer?), and patterns have evolved rapidly — class components → hooks → Server Components — meaning older tutorials may teach approaches the community no longer recommends.
Angular (2016) is Google's complete rewrite of AngularJS. Where React is a library, Angular is a
. It includes routing, HTTP client, form validation, dependency injection, animation, internationalisation, and a CLI that generates and lints project structure — all first-party and integrated.

Angular's opinionated nature is a genuine strength for large enterprise teams: every developer writes code the same way, code reviews are easier, and the upgrade path is predictable. Angular 17+ introduced
— a fine-grained reactivity primitive that replaces much of the RxJS ceremony for simple state, closing the DX gap with React and Vue.

Angular is harder to recommend as a first framework because its learning curve front-loads several advanced concepts simultaneously: TypeScript, decorators, RxJS observables, dependency injection, and Angular's own module system. The reward is a codebase that scales predictably to hundreds of engineers.

Vue (2014) was created by Evan You after leaving Google, designed to be the "progressive" framework: you can sprinkle it into an existing HTML page or build a full SPA with Single File Components (SFCs). Vue 3 introduced the
— a hooks-like system for co-locating related logic — while keeping the older Options API for teams that prefer its structure.
Vue's SFC format is widely considered the most
— HTML markup stays as HTML with well-named directives (
) rather than requiring JSX. Vue's official ecosystem includes Vue Router and Pinia for state, and Nuxt.js for server-side rendering.

Vue is extremely popular in Asia and in certain European markets, but fewer job postings in North America and the UK request it compared to React. Choosing Vue is a sound technical choice but a riskier career one unless you are targeting markets where it is prevalent.
Svelte (2016, now maintained at Vercel) takes the most radical approach: rather than shipping a runtime that interprets your component code in the browser, Svelte
at build time. The result has no virtual DOM and no framework runtime overhead — just targeted DOM updates.

{#if isLoading}

Loading...

{:else}
    {#each users as u (u.id)}
  • {u.name}
    • {/each}
{/if}

{count} users
— replacing the older reactive label syntax. This is a significant API change from Svelte 4, and most existing Svelte tutorials target Svelte 4.

Svelte excels at content sites, marketing pages, and embedded widgets where bundle size and raw performance matter most. SvelteKit provides full-stack conventions on top. The tradeoff: Svelte has the smallest ecosystem of the four, fewer jobs, and fewer large-scale production references to learn from.
Despite different syntaxes, all four frameworks share the same core ideas. Learning React deeply makes picking up the others much faster because the mental models transfer:
UI is built from composable, reusable units with their own state and lifecycle.
Describe what the UI should look like for a given state; the framework handles DOM updates.
Components receive data from parents through props / attributes.
Children notify parents via callbacks or emitted events.
A way to run side effects when component state changes (useEffect / ngOnInit / onMounted / $effect).
All four support conditional display and rendering arrays of items with stable keys.
The biggest technical difference between the four is
how they detect state changes and update the DOM

. React re-runs the component function on each state change and diffs the resulting virtual DOM tree against the previous one. Angular 17+ uses fine-grained Signals that track exactly which template expressions depend on which signal values. Vue uses a reactive proxy system — reading a
inside a computed or template registers a dependency automatically. Svelte does this at compile time by analysing assignments to
React's VDOM diffing is the least efficient approach in theory, but React's reconciler (Fiber) is highly optimized and rarely a practical bottleneck. Where it matters, React provides
React's ecosystem advantage is its size and the quality of competition within it. Every important problem has multiple high-quality solutions that have been battle-tested at scale:
shadcn/ui, MUI, Ant Design, Radix, Chakra
Angular Testing Utils, Vue Testing Library
This course teaches React specifically for three reasons:
React skills are the most directly transferable to employment. More open roles, more tutorials, more community support.

React's minimal surface area means you encounter the fundamental ideas (components, state, effects, context) without a framework hiding them. When you later read Angular code, you can see what its DI is doing. When you read Vue, the Composition API looks familiar.
React Native, React for native desktop via Tauri/Electron, React Server Components — the same mental model covers more deployment targets than any other option.

Learning React makes you a better Vue or Svelte developer later. The converse is also true but less common — React's JSX forces you to understand what the framework is doing rather than relying on magic directives.
If you are choosing a framework for a project rather than for learning, here is a practical decision guide:
Largest job market, most learning resources
Enforced structure, TypeScript, DI patterns familiar from backend
Team wants gentle onboarding, HTML backgrounds
Least cognitive shift from HTML, progressive adoption
Compiler eliminates runtime overhead, SvelteKit for SSR
React Native shares components and patterns
RSC, streaming, file-based routing, Vercel deployment
What Transferable Skills Look Like in Practice

To make the transfers concrete, here is the same pattern — a component that fetches a user and displays a loading / error / success state — in React and then mapped to equivalent idioms in the others:
The Vue Composition API equivalent uses
. The shape of thinking — local state + reactive side effect + derived render — is identical.

Benchmarks show Svelte's compiled output is faster in micro-benchmarks, but real applications are dominated by network latency, database calls, and render logic — not the framework's diffing cost. React scales to million-user products without its VDOM becoming a bottleneck.

Angular has a large, stable corporate user base and receives active investment from Google. Its developer satisfaction scores have improved significantly since Angular 16–17 with the introduction of Signals and standalone components.
"You must pick one and never switch."
Senior engineers regularly move between frameworks. The concepts — reactivity, component lifecycle, data flow, SSR — are the durable knowledge. The syntax is just syntax.
"Vue is easier so you should start with Vue."

Vue's lower friction is real, but starting with React's slightly higher friction pays dividends — JSX exposes you to JavaScript-as-template, which is a portable skill. Vue's template directives are easier to start but harder to debug when behavior is unexpected.
Interoperability: Using React Alongside Other Frameworks

In practice many teams mix frameworks at the micro-frontend or page level. A legacy Angular monolith might ship a new feature as a React widget embedded via a custom element. A WordPress site might use React for a dynamic product configurator on one page while leaving the rest server-rendered PHP. Understanding this interoperability context helps you reason about the real-world landscape you will encounter on teams.
React components can be rendered to a custom element wrapper using
from any script on the page. This means a React-built search widget can live inside an Angular shell or a Vue page without either framework being aware of the other:

The reverse — embedding Angular or Vue components inside a React tree — is also possible via refs and imperative mounts, though it requires more coordination. This island-of-React pattern is common during incremental migrations away from older frameworks.
Framework vs Library: Why the Distinction Matters for Architecture

The library-vs-framework distinction is not just philosophical — it has real architectural consequences. Angular's opinionated structure means you get predictability in exchange for flexibility. React's minimal core means you get flexibility in exchange for decision fatigue early on.

A React application's architecture is defined by the team rather than the framework. Two React apps can be architecturally completely different: one using Redux + React Router + class components (circa 2019), another using Zustand + TanStack Router + Server Components (2025). An Angular application, by contrast, will look structurally familiar regardless of the team that built it.

For a solo developer or a small team this flexibility is a strength. For an organisation onboarding dozens of engineers a year, Angular's enforced conventions reduce code-review overhead. There is no universally right answer — it depends on team size, rate of change, and how much architectural governance is needed.
Bundle size affects how fast your app loads on slow networks. Here are approximate gzipped sizes for each framework's runtime (2024):
Shared across all React in the page; cached after first visit
Ivy tree-shakes well; grows with features used
Smaller runtime than React; ships a VDOM
Compiler removes runtime; grows with component code

For most applications the framework runtime is not the dominant cost — application code, images, fonts, and third-party scripts dwarf it. React's 42 KB is loaded once, cached indefinitely by the browser, and split away from application code. Bundle size becomes meaningful when targeting very low-end mobile devices on 3G networks, or when shipping micro-widgets embedded in third-party sites where no shared CDN version is available.
One of the most interesting trends in frontend 2022–2025 is that all four frameworks are converging on similar primitives:
is effectively a signal. Svelte's runes are compiler-mediated signals. React is experimenting with a future signals primitive.
Server rendering as a first-class concern.
All four have official SSR stories. React's RSC, Angular Universal, Nuxt (Vue), SvelteKit — all address the blank-screen problem.
Next.js, SvelteKit, Nuxt, and Angular 17+ all support file-based routing conventions at the meta-framework level.
React Suspense, Vue's async components with
, SvelteKit's streaming responses — all four now support streaming the page as data arrives.

This convergence means that skills learned in any one framework become more and more transferable. The competition between frameworks benefits developers — each borrows the best ideas from the others, raising the quality of the entire ecosystem.
When choosing a framework for a multi-year project, community health and long-term maintenance matter as much as current feature parity:

is backed by Meta and sustained by a massive open-source ecosystem. It has maintained backwards compatibility since 2013 while introducing new capabilities. React 18–19's concurrent features were shipped without breaking existing code.
is backed by Google with a stable long-term support (LTS) policy. Each major version is supported for 18 months. The Angular team provides migration schematics to automate most breaking changes.

is community-driven with Evan You as the primary maintainer and a small core team. It is financially supported through GitHub Sponsors and corporate adopters. The v2 → v3 migration was slower and more disruptive than Angular's tooled upgrades, but the community has fully transitioned.

is backed by Vercel and its creator Rich Harris. The Svelte 4 → 5 runes redesign was a significant breaking change — the framework is still maturing its API stability story. For greenfield projects this is fine; for long-lived codebases it warrants a closer look at upgrade cost.

The testing approach differs slightly between frameworks, but the underlying principles — unit test logic, integration-test user interactions, avoid testing implementation details — are universal. React Testing Library (RTL) popularised the philosophy of testing from the user's perspective, and that philosophy has now spread to
for creating isolated component environments and
for querying the DOM, though many Angular teams also add
on top. The key difference is Angular tests often need to configure providers and modules, whereas React and Vue tests are more self-contained.

Playwright and Cypress for end-to-end testing are framework-agnostic — they interact with the rendered DOM via browser automation, so the same test suite structure applies regardless of which framework built the page.

All four frameworks support TypeScript, but with different degrees of integration. Angular requires TypeScript — you cannot write an Angular component in plain JavaScript in the modern CLI. React, Vue, and Svelte treat TypeScript as opt-in, and their core APIs work equally well in both.
React with TypeScript annotates props using interfaces or type aliases:
generic syntax achieves the same prop typing inside SFCs. Svelte 5's
rune destructures a typed object. In all cases, TypeScript catches prop-type mismatches at compile time — a major advantage on larger codebases.
Each framework provides different tools for preventing unnecessary re-renders or computations:
Functions defined in setup are stable
Functions defined in script are stable
React's optimisation model is more explicit — you must reach for

when needed. Vue and Svelte optimise automatically based on reactive tracking. Angular 17+ with OnPush + Signals achieves similar auto-memoisation. React's React Compiler project (experimental in 2024) aims to automate these optimisations at build time, closing this ergonomics gap.
; Vue is progressive; Svelte is a compiler.
All four share the same component, props, state, and effect concepts — syntax differs, mental model does not.
React leads on job market size and ecosystem breadth; Angular leads on opinionated structure for large teams; Vue leads on approachability; Svelte leads on compiled bundle size.
Mastering any one framework deeply transfers to the others — the fundamentals are the same problem solved with different trade-offs.
